macro_summary: Friday's session was dominated by the June payrolls miss (57k vs. 129k expected), which sharply reduced rate-hike bets and sent gold surging 2.6% to $4,187. The Dollar weakened to 100.84, while WTI edged up to $68.78 as US-Iran Doha talks concluded with 'positive progress,' further easing the Hormuz risk premium. Tech was the clear laggard, with the Nasdaq (QQQ) -1.6% and SOXX -5.6% as Meta's AI cloud ambitions intensified hyperscaler-competition fears, triggering a broad semiconductor selloff.
commodities_note: WTI recovered to $68.78 (+1.6% daily) as Doha talks tempered supply fears, but remains down 28.4% over the past month – the Hormuz premium is nearly gone. Gold at $4,187 is testing new highs on a combination of a softer Dollar and dovish repricing after the payrolls miss. US10Y held at 4.48%, steady for now.

singapore.summary: The three banks remain uniformly extended, with DBS, OCBC, and UOB all 6–8% above their 50‑day averages. The NIM compression headwind persists: US10Y at 4.48% is above the 4.20% threshold, and the MAS has not eased. Without a catalyst – either a rate pivot or a MAS shift in October – the cluster is in a waiting pattern. OCBC is the relative outperform on fundamentals, but all three are mechanically blocked.
singapore.thesis_status: Structurally impaired – awaiting NIM relief catalyst.
singapore.key_development: OCBC cut its oil price forecasts as Hormuz shipments recover (per InvestorsHub, July 2), a modest positive for its energy‑linked loan book.

semiconductors.summary: Friday’s -5.6% SOXX rout, driven by Meta’s AI cloud ambitions, hit the entire cluster. Only TSM and ASML retained WATCH signals, while NVDA, AMD, MU, and others are mired in CAUTION. The selloff was broad, but the damage was worst in mid‑cap names (TSEM -10.4%, LITE -8.8%). The cluster lacks a near‑term catalyst to reverse the sentiment; we’re in a regime where individual stock selection matters less than sector beta.
semiconductors.thesis_status: Structurally bullish, tactically impaired – oversold but no catalyst.
semiconductors.key_development: SK Hynix’s $29.4B ADR listing (per Simply Wall St.) is a structural positive, but it didn’t save the stock from a -16.9% weekly drop.

big_tech.summary: The divergence within BigTech is stark: MSFT surged last week (+10.7% 5‑day) on hopes it would benefit from Meta’s cloud entry, but the move was too fast and is now blocked. GOOG and AMZN are stuck below their 50‑day averages, with no immediate catalysts. PLTR is similarly blocked after a +20.5% 5‑day run. The cluster is a mixed bag – some overbought, some drifting – with earnings in late July as the next reset.
big_tech.thesis_status: Mixed – MSFT overbought, AMZN/GOOG searching for direction.
big_tech.key_development: Meta’s AI cloud push (per Yahoo Finance, July 1) is the defining event of the week, raising competitive fears across the BigTech names.

interconnected.name: Coherent
interconnected.reason: Recipient of $2B NVIDIA photonics investment alongside LITE; its 5% decline on July 2 mirrors the optics selloff.
interconnected.entry_note: Add to watchlist if LITE finds support above 642 and Coherent retests its 200‑day SMA on above‑average volume.

interconnected.name: Marvell Technology
interconnected.reason: Beneficiary of the same AI‑optical interconnect trend as LITE/COHR, with a strong custom ASIC business – read‑through from NVDA/AVGO demand signals.
interconnected.entry_note: Revisit after TSM’s July 16 earnings for CoWoS demand commentary; a positive read would boost MRVL’s growth case.

interconnected.name: Samsung Electronics
interconnected.reason: HBM3E qualification struggles have tightened the HBM duopoly, directly lifting SK Hynix and Micron. Recent labor unrest (per Reuters, May 14) adds supply risk.
interconnected.entry_note: Add to watchlist if Samsung secures a major HBM3E qualification from a hyperscaler, confirming its 14nm DRAM yield improvement.

active_risks: US‑Iran Doha talks conclude with 'positive progress,' easing Hormuz supply fears and sending WTI down 28% monthly.
Fed Chair Warsh signals declining inflation risks and potential AI job creation, but the June jobs miss (57k vs 129k) raises doubt on a near‑term rate hike.
US‑China tech tensions persist: Pentagon blacklist aims at China’s AI, while Intel is reportedly restarting older chip supply to China, hinting at some thawing.
ECB’s Lagarde cannot rule out early departure, adding uncertainty to euro area monetary policy.
geopolitical.market_impact: The risk‑off rotation that began on Friday hit semis hard (SOXX -5.6%), while gold soared 2.6% as rate‑hike bets collapsed. The flight to safety lifted defensive plays and hurt tech, particularly the AI‑exposed names that had rallied on capex enthusiasm. The softening oil price and the fading Hormuz premium are a double‑edged sword: they ease inflation fears but also remove the emergency demand that had supported some commodity‑linked equities.
geopolitical.new_since_yesterday: June payrolls came in at 57k, a stinging miss vs 129k expected, triggering a sharp repricing of Fed rate expectations. US‑Iran Doha talks concluded with 'positive progress' according to Qatar, the clearest de‑escalation signal yet.

base.description: Hormuz reopens steadily, oil stabilizes near $65‑70. The Fed holds rates steady, and the labor market cools further. The AI capex narrative remains intact, but the near‑term selloff in semis persists until earnings validate spending.

optimistic.description: A rapid Hormuz normalization and a soft landing prompt a Fed pivot by Q1 2027. Semis rebound on renewed AI optimism, and the cluster returns to a broad uptrend led by NVDA, TSM, and ASML.

pessimistic.description: Hormuz tensions re‑escalate, WTI spikes back above $90, and the Fed is forced to hike once. The AI capex bubble fears intensify, triggering a deeper semis selloff and a flight to gold and defensive assets.

wildcard.description: Meta’s AI cloud business gains rapid traction, disrupting hyperscaler pricing power and triggering a structural reduction in third‑party GPU demand. AI hardware stocks (NVDA, AMD, MU) face a prolonged de‑rating, while MSFT and AMZN benefit from a competitive moat.

geopolitical.portfolio_action: Hold cash, no new entries until the semis selloff stabilizes and individual names show clear support tests. Monitor the July 14 CPI – a benign print could loosen the CAUTION grip on extended bank names.

portfolio_snapshot.risk_posture: 7 of 29 names are hard‑blocked on technical extension, and another 15 are in CAUTION due to valuation or fundamentals. The semis cluster is in a risk‑off spiral driven by Meta’s cloud ambitions, but a few quality names (TSM, ASML) hold WATCH status. Cash is king while the cluster resets.

calibration_insights.note: Block is output-only — assembled post-LLM from signal_evaluation_log outcomes and never injected into the analysis prompt.
